title: Rally portfolio hierarchy & comparison
topic: Portfolio hierarchy modeling in Rally (CA / Broadcom Agile Central) — comparative view across Jira Advanced Roadmaps, Azure DevOps, Aha!
summary: Rally uses one PortfolioItem base type with admin-configurable sub-types (Theme/Initiative/Feature, up to 5 levels P1–P5); strategy↔execution boundary is the single PortfolioItem field on User Story; rollup is stored on write asynchronously.
content:
Contents
  1. Summary
  2. Key Findings
  3. Detailed Analysis
  4. Comparative View — Rally vs Jira Advanced Roadmaps vs Azure DevOps vs Aha!
  5. Sources
  6. Gaps & Limitations

Summary

Rally (CA / Broadcom Agile Central) models its portfolio hierarchy as a single base type, PortfolioItem, with admin-configurable sub-types (Theme / Initiative / Feature by default; up to five levels P1–P5) addressed at the API as separate slashed endpoints (PortfolioItem/Feature, PortfolioItem/Initiative, etc.). The strategy/execution boundary sits squarely at the lowest portfolio level — only that bottom level can be referenced from a User Story (HierarchicalRequirement) via the PortfolioItem field. Comparable tools (Jira Advanced Roadmaps, Azure DevOps, Aha!) take noticeably different stances on configurability, rollup, and the strategy boundary; the comparison table at the end captures the contrasts.

Key Findings

  • One base type, type-discriminated sub-endpoints. Rally uses a single PortfolioItem abstract type plus per-level sub-types exposed as PortfolioItem/Theme, PortfolioItem/Initiative, PortfolioItem/Feature in WSAPI v2.0 [1][8].
  • Hierarchy is admin-configurable, not a hard-coded enum. Workspaces can rename types, change ID prefixes, and configure up to five levels (P1–P5). Reordering requires renaming via temporary intermediates because the order itself is fixed by level number; deletion is only allowed if no items exist at that type [4][7].
  • Single parent per item; levels cannot be skipped from below. Each PortfolioItem has one Parent; stories attach only to the lowest level via PortfolioItem; the Feature field on a story is read-only and dynamically named after the lowest level [2][3].
  • Strategy/execution boundary is the lowest PortfolioItem level. Stories (HierarchicalRequirement) and Defects sit on the execution side; PortfolioItem objects on the strategy side; the link is a regular FK on the lowest level only [2][3].
  • Story-to-story parenting also exists, mutually exclusive with PortfolioItem assignment. Setting PortfolioItem on a child story clears its Parent and severs the chain; cascading propagation pushes a portfolio assignment down through child stories [2].
  • Rollup is stored on write (asynchronously). Read-only fields like PercentDoneByStoryCount, PercentDoneByStoryPlanEstimate, AcceptedLeafStoryCount, LeafStoryPlanEstimateTotal, and LastRollupDate are aggregated by background processes; a manual "Correct rollup discrepancy" action exists when rollups lag [5].
  • Reparenting is allowed but constrained. Moving a story to a different feature (or feature to a different initiative) cuts the original chain and creates a new sub-hierarchy; lock-down work rules can prohibit reparenting outright [2][9].
  • Orphans are permitted but discouraged. True orphans are stories with both Parent = null and PortfolioItem = null; Rally does not block creation but best-practice docs warn against story-only parenting and recommend always parenting to a portfolio item [10].
  • Subtree filtering is via Portfolio Kanban / Portfolio Items / Tree Grid. Users pick a level and scope; Tree Grid components show a node + descendants; community apps (PortfolioItem-Tree, custom-grid-with-deep-export) extend this for arbitrary-depth export [11][12].

Detailed Analysis

1. Schema / data model

Rally uses a single base type PortfolioItem from which Theme, Initiative, and Feature inherit. Internally this is best described as single-table-with-type-discriminator on the persistence side, but the WSAPI surfaces sub-types as separate slashed endpoints so that callers can query (and create) them individually [1][8]:

GET /slm/webservice/v2.0/portfolioitem/feature
GET /slm/webservice/v2.0/portfolioitem/initiative
GET /slm/webservice/v2.0/portfolioitem/theme

Custom sub-types created by an admin become new endpoints automatically — which is why Broadcom warns admins not to give a custom level a name that collides with an existing WSAPI endpoint [4][7]. Rally documents 4 standard sub-types (Theme, Strategy, Initiative, Feature) [1].

2. Strategy / execution boundary

The boundary is the lowest portfolio level. Stories never reference a Theme or an Initiative directly; they reference whatever the lowest configured PortfolioItem level is, via a single FK called PortfolioItem on HierarchicalRequirement. A second field, dynamically named after the lowest level (e.g. Feature), exists on the story but is read-only — it shadows PortfolioItem and exists for traceability and reporting [2][3].

The implication is that the strategy zone (Theme → Initiative → Feature) is fully expressed via Parent links between PortfolioItems, while the execution zone (Story → Task → Defect) is anchored to that single lowest-level handoff field [2][3].

3. Parent-child FK rules

  • Single parent. Each PortfolioItem has one nullable Parent; multi-parenting is not supported [3].
  • Levels can’t be skipped from below. A story cannot point at an Initiative or Theme; only the lowest level [2].
  • Levels can be skipped between PortfolioItems. The docs do not require an Initiative to have a Theme parent — nullable parent is allowed all the way up [3].
  • Mutually exclusive parenting on stories. A HierarchicalRequirement can have a story Parent or a PortfolioItem, not both; setting PortfolioItem clears Parent and makes it a root story in its chain [2].
  • Cross-project allowed within workspace. Children of a portfolio item don’t need to live in the same project as the portfolio item; one set of portfolio item types per workspace [13].

4. Rollup mechanics

Rollups are stored on write rather than computed on read. The Portfolio Item Fields page documents these read-only fields [5]:

  • PercentDoneByStoryCount — accepted stories ÷ total stories
  • PercentDoneByStoryPlanEstimate — accepted points ÷ total points
  • AcceptedLeafStoryCount, AcceptedLeafStoryPlanEstimateTotal
  • LeafStoryCount, LeafStoryPlanEstimateTotal
  • UnEstimatedLeafStoryCount
  • ActualStartDate, ActualEndDate (auto-populated from child transitions)
  • LastRollupDate — system-generated timestamp; manual "Correct rollup discrepancy" action available when calculations lag [5]

The async nature is acknowledged by the explicit "correct rollup discrepancy" affordance — rollups are eventually consistent, not transactional. The Portfolio Kanban shows aggregated WIP and flow at the level you select, with cycle-time and cumulative-flow charts driven by the same rolled-up fields [11].

5. Reparenting

Reparenting a story between features is allowed via the PortfolioItem field (the UI’s "Parent" field for stories edits the story’s story-parent, not its feature). When you reassign, the story is "cut" from its old chain — child stories of the moved story go with it as a new sub-hierarchy [2]. Story points and history are preserved on the moved item; Rally maintains full revision history for every artifact, and Lookback API exposes historical state.

Rally also offers lock-down work rules on portfolio items, which can disable add/delete/reparent of children regardless of project scope [9].

6. Orphan handling

Orphans are permitted but flagged as anti-pattern. The canonical orphan query is (Parent = null) AND (PortfolioItem = null) [10]. Best-practice docs say teams should always parent stories to a portfolio item rather than to other stories [10]. Rally also released the ability to require a parent on user stories and portfolio items at the workspace level — making orphan creation impossible if enabled [14].

7. Subtree filtering UX

Three first-class affordances exist:

  • Portfolio Kanban — pick a level (Theme / Initiative / Feature); board shows that level only with WIP per state [11].
  • Portfolio Items page / Tree Grid — expand-collapse hierarchy; node-and-descendants drill-in; arrow controls for expand/contract on Portfolio Timeline Roadmap [12].
  • Custom Grid + community apps — PortfolioItem-Tree and custom-grid-with-deep-export open-source apps extend this with deep export and arbitrary-depth tree views [12].

8. Custom level naming

Workspace admins can rename Theme/Initiative/Feature, change ID prefixes (e.g. T1, T2 for Theme), and add or delete levels — without a schema migration. Constraints [7]:

  • You can rename name and ID prefix but not the order; reorder by renaming through temporary names.
  • A type can only be deleted if it has no associated portfolio items.
  • Up to five levels (P1 – P5); default workspaces ship with two (Feature P1, Initiative P2).
  • Avoid names that collide with existing WSAPI endpoints — new types create new endpoints [4].
  • Active users viewing the type may see a transient error until they refresh.

Comparative View — Rally vs Jira Advanced Roadmaps vs Azure DevOps vs Aha!

Configurability

  • Rally: 1–5 admin-configurable levels; rename/add/delete via Setup; new types become new WSAPI endpoints [4][7].
  • Jira Advanced Roadmaps (now "Plans"): Default Epic (level 1) and Story (level 0); custom levels can be added above Epic only; you cannot insert levels below Epic or between existing levels. Premium / Enterprise only [15].
  • Azure DevOps: Three system processes (Agile, Scrum, CMMI) define Epic / Feature / [Story|PBI|Requirement] / Task with Bug placement varying. Custom Work Item Types are addable via Inherited Process; you can add a new portfolio backlog level but cannot insert one between existing system levels, and a WIT belongs to only one backlog level [16][17].
  • Aha! Roadmaps: Goal, Initiative, Epic, Feature, Requirement; initiatives can roll up to higher initiatives across up to five levels; features can be converted to other record types (initiative, requirement, epic) so the "type" is convertible per record rather than schema-mutated [18][19].

Rollup

  • Rally: Stored on write, async, with read-only rollup fields (PercentDoneByStoryCount, LeafStoryPlanEstimateTotal, LastRollupDate) and a manual recompute affordance [5].
  • Jira Advanced Roadmaps: Computed on read at plan-view level; story-point sums, dates, and progress aggregate across the plan’s configured hierarchy [15].
  • Azure DevOps: Rollup columns on backlogs are configurable; can show progress bars, descendant counts, totals of any numeric field; supported across portfolio backlogs [20][21].
  • Aha!: Initiative-to-initiative rollup up to five levels; feature/epic/initiative all support estimates; aggregation is built into the Hierarchy Report [18][19].

Strategy / execution boundary

  • Rally: Hard split — PortfolioItem vs HierarchicalRequirement/Defect/Task. Single FK at the lowest level [2][3].
  • Jira Advanced Roadmaps: Soft split — everything is a Jira Issue; hierarchy is a configured ordering of issue types; parent-child via standard issue links [15].
  • Azure DevOps: Soft split — everything is a Work Item with parent-child links; Microsoft documents that you should only create parent-child links one level deep between items of different categories (don’t parent stories to stories) [22].
  • Aha!: Distinct record types per level (Goal/Initiative/Epic/Feature/Requirement) with type-specific behavior; conversion is supported between certain types [19].

Comparison Table — the 8 Rally questions across all four tools

Question Rally Jira Advanced Roadmaps Azure DevOps Aha! Roadmaps
Schema / data model Single PortfolioItem base + sub-type endpoints (PortfolioItem/Feature etc.); story is separate HierarchicalRequirement [1][8] One Issue table; hierarchy is a configured ordering of issue types [15] One Work Item table; type discriminated; parent-child via link types [22] Distinct record types per level (Goal, Initiative, Epic, Feature, Requirement) [18]
Strategy / execution boundary Hard: PortfolioItem vs HierarchicalRequirement/Defect/Task [2][3] Soft: all issues, hierarchy is config [15] Soft: all work items, parent-child links [22] Distinct record types but flexible conversion [19]
Multi-parent / level-skipping Single parent; story attaches only to lowest level; story-parent and PortfolioItem mutually exclusive [2][3] Single parent; level skipping not enforced — you can place issues directly under any configured level [15] Single parent recommended; Microsoft warns against same-category parent-child [22] Single parent per record; flexible mapping (epics can map to initiatives, epics, or features) [19]
Rollup compute (read vs write) Stored on write, async; LastRollupDate; manual correct-discrepancy [5] Computed on read at plan view [15] Computed via rollup columns; configurable per backlog [20][21] Built into Hierarchy Report; up to 5 levels of initiatives [18]
Reparenting effects Cuts old chain into new sub-hierarchy; lock-down rules can disable [2][9] Drag-and-drop in plan view; recomputed at next plan refresh [15] Standard parent-link change; rollups recompute [22] Re-link with conversion if crossing record types [19]
Orphan handling Allowed; query Parent=null AND PortfolioItem=null; can be required at workspace level [10][14] Allowed; soft warning at plan level [15] Allowed; surfaced via Parents view [22] Allowed; report-driven discovery [18]
Subtree filter UX Portfolio Kanban (level pick) + Tree Grid + custom apps [11][12] Plan view scope filters by epic/initiative [15] Backlog "Parents" view + Boards filter [22] Hierarchy report; pivots/lists/charts [18]
Custom level naming Admin-configurable; up to 5 levels; no migration; renames flow on refresh [4][7] Admin-configurable above Epic only; Premium / Enterprise [15] Inherited Process; add WITs and backlog levels but cannot insert between system levels [16][17] Records convertible between types per-instance; no schema migration [19]

Gaps & Limitations

  • The canonical Rally WSAPI object reference page (rally-help/reference/rally-web-services-api.html) is a thin overview that points at dynamic in-product help; deep field-level schema is only fully visible to authenticated tenants. Field details here are stitched from the public Portfolio Item Fields and User Story Fields pages plus the knowledge.broadcom.com KB articles, not from a single canonical schema dump.
  • Rally’s persistence layer (single-table-with-discriminator vs per-sub-type tables) is inferred from API surface behavior — Broadcom does not publish DB-level details. The WSAPI’s slashed sub-endpoints and behavior of custom types creating new endpoints make a discriminated single-store the most plausible model, but that is an inference, not a published fact.
  • Aha!’s public docs describe the conceptual hierarchy clearly, but the underlying record-table model (one table vs many) is not documented in their public knowledge base — flagged as inference-only above.
  • Specific behavior of Rally’s Lookback API for hierarchy snapshots was not fetched in detail; the Lookback API exists and exposes historical state, but its query model for hierarchy traversal was not deep-dived.
  • Jira Advanced Roadmaps was renamed to "Plans" in Jira Premium/Enterprise; some older Atlassian docs still use the legacy name and feature parity has shifted — treat the comparison row as a snapshot of mid-2025 behavior.
